coredata.dc:title: Ellagic acid-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots for ultrasensitive and selective detection of mercuric ions via quenching
coredata.dc:creator: Joseph, Julin
Anappara, Aji A.
coredata.dc:description: The authors report on a strategy for the fabrication of water soluble carbon quantum dots (CDs) functionalized with ellagic acid. They also demonstrate highly selective detection of Hg2+ ions in aqueous media via fluorescence quenching. The CDs were prepared via a one-step technique using tannic acid as the carbon source and directly surface functionalized during nanoparticle synthesis. Following photoexcitation at 210nm they exhibit excitation-independent emission that peaks at 340nm. The CDs display strong affinity for Hg2+ and binding result in quenching of fluorescence. This effect can be used to quantify Hg2+ in concentration down to 10pM and with detection limit as low as 0.24pM. The ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ ions is demonstrated by analyzing Hg2+-spiked water and tap-water samples.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of analytes in the aqueous phase

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of the synthesized CDs. The inset shows a high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of an individual carbon dot indicating the typical lattice constant. (b) Size-distribution of the CDs as measured from the TEM image. (c) AFM image of the CDs exhibiting quasi-spherical nanoparticles. (d) The height profiles of the CDs, as measured using AFM.

(a) Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral data for the CDs measured by the KBr pellet method. (b) Electrospray ionization mass spectra (ESI-MS) of the nanoparticles synthesized through the micro-wave hydrothermal method.

a) UV–visible absorption spectra of aqueous dispersion the CDs, collected with water as reference. Inset: Calculation of the band-edge using Tauc plot with the UV–vis absorption data. b) Contour plots of the excitation–emission photoluminescence (PL) matrix of the CDs dispersed in water. The Raleigh–scattering peaks are marked with the white dashed lines.

a) Comparative bar plot summarizing the relative variation in luminescence quenching with the presence of different metal ions. The concentrations of the metal ions are fixed at 5µM. The peak amplitude of the luminescence for different samples is normalized with the blank sample. Error bars are added based on the standard deviation of three replicate measurements. b) The PL spectra with Hg2+ concentrations in picomolar (pM) range. Corresponding Stern-Volmer plot is displayed in the inset.

head.ce:title: Ellagic acid-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots for ultrasensitive and selective detection of mercuric ions via quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC), Kerala 673601, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology Calicut (NITC)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The authors report on a strategy for the fabrication of water soluble carbon quantum dots (CDs) functionalized with ellagic acid. They also demonstrate highly selective detection of Hg2+ ions in aqueous media via fluorescence quenching. The CDs were prepared via a one-step technique using tannic acid as the carbon source and directly surface functionalized during nanoparticle synthesis. Following photoexcitation at 210nm they exhibit excitation-independent emission that peaks at 340nm. The CDs display strong affinity for Hg2+ and binding result in quenching of fluorescence. This effect can be used to quantify Hg2+ in concentration down to 10pM and with detection limit as low as 0.24pM. The ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ ions is demonstrated by analyzing Hg2+-spiked water and tap-water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury and its compounds are known to be extremely toxic and ubiquitously distributed in environment [1]. Hg2+ is highly water-soluble and the most stable inorganic metal ion. Through microbial-mediated methylation, Hg2+ ions get converted to methyl mercury; a well-known neurotoxin [2–4]. The continuous exposure of mercury through drinking water leads to its accumulation in muscles and liver of humans and also causes serious damages to DNA and central nervous system [5–7]. The exposure limit for inorganic mercury (Hg2+) in drinking water is set at 2 ppb (2µg/L) by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US-EPA) [8]. In the past decades there have been intense researches dedicated to the development of selective and sensitive mercury detection methods [9,10]. Even though traditional and well established analytical methods like Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (AAS), Inductively Coupled Plasma-Mass Spectroscopy (ICP-MS) and Inductively Coupled Plasma-Atomic Emission Spectroscopy (ICP-AES) and electrochemical techniques have good sensitivity (down to sub-µg/L or ng/L) [9,10]. However, these methods have various drawbacks such as high-cost, lack of mobility and/or the requirement of significant sample preparation protocols. Hg2+ ions have no optical-spectroscopic signature because of its closed-shell d10 configuration which limits a direct spectroscopic detection of the pollutant [11]. Thus, probes based on changes in the fluorescence (intensity or wavelength) induced by the presence of mercuric ions are particularly attractive due to its simplicity of use and cost-effectiveness [10,12]. Even though variety of remarkable probes based on small organic molecules, semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoparticles have been used for the detection of Hg2+ in drinking water, but the wide application of these probes are limited due to their complex synthesis and/or toxicity [12,13].

Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CDs), are sub-10nm carbon nanoparticles, with sp2-hybridised graphite core and surface-functionalized with several groups. Thanks to the unique features of CDs such as non-toxicity, excellent aqueous dispersity, lack of photo-blinking, chemical inertness and remarkable emission quantum yield, they have been used in wide range of applications such as photovoltaics, photocatalysis, energy conversion/storage, optoelectronics, bioimaging/biosensing and chemical sensing [14–17]. Interesting optoelectronic features of CDs like UV-absorption, luminescence in blue-green region and excitation-wavelength dependent/independent emission from the size-quantization in the graphitic nanodomains in the core, in conjunction with the surface functionality. The UV-absorption by the CDs may be assigned to the interband electronic excitations resulting from the confined sp2 conjugation in the carbon core. Whereas the photoluminescence, may be due to the de-excitation of the photo-generated carriers in the emissive sites formed by the hybridization of the carbon network and the functional groups [18]. The fabrication strategies of CDs are generally classified into top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down synthesis relies on the fragmentation of the bulk graphitic materials until the nanoparticles become luminescent. But in bottom-up route, molecular precursors are carbonized at suitable conditions [16,17]. In the latter method, during the formation of CDs, the surface-functional groups of the precursors and/or the reactants are intrinsically transferred to the unsatisfied valences of the surface carbon atoms. Hence, not only the internal characteristics of the carbon nanoparticles (such as particle-size, crystallinity of the graphitic core, elemental composition, dispersity in solvents), the chemical and optoelectronic attributes also are strongly dependent on the synthesis strategies and the choice of precursors. In order to realize CDs with specific and custom-tailored properties; the steps of nanoparticle preparation as well as the surface-functionalization of the CDs are integrated to a single process known as Synthesis-modification integration [19–25]. This technique relies on identifying a precursor which can act as the carbon source and with suitable functional groups. Because the functional groups on the precursor will decorate the surface of the nanoparticles during the synthesis.

In this work we report a one-step fabrication of UV-photoluminescent, ellagic acid-functionalized carbon quantum dots through synthesis-modification integration strategy using tannic acid (TA) as the carbon source. The precursor, TA when mixed with sulphuric acid (without any external heat), undergo acid-catalyzed hydrolysis and further dehydration to form carbon dots. The resulting CDs exhibit excitation-wavelength independent emission at 340nm, upon irradiation with UV light (excitation wavelengths in the range 210–350nm). TA is selected as the precursor because of two reasons: (i) TA is a plant-polyphenol with carbohydrate (glucose) core and with ten gallic acid (GA) units [21]; primarily, the pyranose glucose ring is a good carbon source and (ii) crucially, gallic acid/digallic units can be functionalized on the surface of carbon dots during the synthesis. It have already been reported that two gallic acid units may couple to form ellagic acid (EA) derivatives in the presence of oxidizing agents [26]. The representative chemical structures of TA, GA and EA are shown in Fig. S1.

Herein this work, H2SO4 plays the role of dehydrating agent (converting the glucose core of TA to nanographitic clusters); as well as oxidizing agent (in the formation of ellagic acid derivatives, through the coupling of GA units). The physico-chemical characterizations of the synthesized nanoparticles indicate that they are surface-functionalized with ellagic acid derivatives. The CDs reported in this work were utilized for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions.

Tannic acid and sulphuric acid were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. Ethyl acetate was purchased from Merck chemicals. Dialysis tubing with molecular weight cut-off 1kDa was purchased from Spectrum Laboratories, USA. The deionized water from Labostar TWF water purification system (18.2MΩcm, Siemens Ultrapure Water Systems) was used for all experiments.

In a typical experiment, 0.125g of tannic acid was added to 10mL conc. H2SO4 and was kept under stirring at ambient conditions for 5min. After mixing, the solution turned yellow to wine-red color. In order to collect nanoparticles from the dark mixture, the resulting solution was further dissolved in 100mL de-ionized water and was then extracted using ethyl acetate. The solvent was removed under reduced pressure using a rotavapor and the resulting powder was re-dispersed in 5mL de-ionized water. The solution was then transferred to a dialysis bag and was dialyzed against ultrapure water for 24h under stirring conditions. The sample inside the dialysis tube was collected and the water was removed by continuous vacuum heating. The remaining powder was used for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of analytes in the aqueous phase
ce:section.ce:para:
The stock solutions of 5 × 10−4 M of analytes such as HgCl2, MgCl2, CuCl2, NiCl2, CdCl2, CaCl2, ZnCl2, FeCl2, CrCl2, PbCl2, L-Alanine, L-cysteine, L-Histidine, L-Leucine, L-Lycine, L-Phenyl alanine, L-Tyrosine, L-Valine, Bovine serum albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin were prepared by dissolving appropriate amounts in a 100mL volumetric flask, which were then diluted for further measurements. 3mL of the aqueous solution of the nanoparticles having concentration 0.13mg/mL was taken in a cuvette and the analytes were added using a micropipette by measuring an accurate amount in such a way that the final concentrations of the analytes in the solution were in the micromolar (µM), nanomolar (nM), picomolar (pM) levels. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using the equation,
LOD
=
3 σ
S
, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and s is the slope of the calibration curve. The excitation wavelength used for all sensing study is 210nm.

A drop of aqueous dispersion of CDs was carefully placed on the copper grid and was dried at ambient conditions for Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) characterization. TEM and high resolution TEM (HRTEM) were performed on JEOL, JEM-2100F at operating voltage of 200kV. The morphological characterizations were performed by atomic force microscope (AFM) using Park XE-100. The samples for AFM measurement were prepared by drop-casting nanoparticles solution on to a mica sheet. 1H NMR was recorded on Bruker Advance ΙII Spectrometer, after dispersing the CDs in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Electrospray Ionization mass spectra (ESI-MS) were recorded using Bruker Micro TOF-QII Mass Spectrometer. The FT-IR spectra of the samples were measured using the KBr pellet method in Thermo Nicolet, Avatar 370 Spectrophotometer with a resolution of about 4cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed using Al Kα source, having energy of 1486.6eV at room temperature using SPECS Surface Nano Analysis system. The samples for XPS were prepared by coating the nanoparticle dispersion on a silica substrate. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement of the CDs was recorded in the 2θ range from 5° to 85°, on a Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer provided with a Cu Kα radiation source, with λ = 1.54056A°(operating at 45kV and 200mA). The UV–Visible absorption spectra were recorded using Perkin Elmer Lambda-35 with resolution of about 1nm. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using PerkinElmer LS 45 Spectrophotometer with 10nm slit width. The fluorescence life-time measurements were performed using Horiba Fluoromax-4, using time correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique.

Fig. 1a represents typical TEM image of the CDs. The nanoparticles are well separated and apparently spherical; and have a size distribution with an average size of about 3nm as represented in Fig. 1b. The nanoparticles were further examined by HRTEM. The inset of Fig. 1b provides the HRTEM image of a typical nanoparticle, which reveals a graphitic-like layered structure with an inter-layer separation of about 0.2nm. Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) pattern of the nanoparticles measured using TEM and is reported in Fig. S2, which also provides an interlayer separation of ~ 0.2nm. The morphology and height profile of nanoparticles were measured using atomic force microscopy (AFM). The AFM specimen was prepared by dropping aqueous dispersion of nanoparticles on to the mica substrate, followed by the evaporation of the solvent under IR lamp. As shown in Fig. 1c and Fig. 1d the representative AFM imaging results indicated that the synthesized nanoparticles have a height of around 3nm. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement of the CDs is presented in Fig. S3. The XRD data exhibits a broad peak corresponding to 2θ value of 26.6°. The broadness of the peak is an indication of weak crystalline nature of the CDs. Using the Bragg's equation,
n λ = 2 d . sin
θ
, the interlayer separation (d-spacing) of the CDs was estimated to be ~ 0.33nm which corresponds to the (002) plane of the graphite.

The functional groups on the CDs were studied through FTIR (Fourier transform Infrared spectroscopy). The FTIR spectra of the sample ( Fig. 2a) displayed prominent peaks as detailed below. The broad absorption band around 3495cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of hydrogen bonded –OH groups. The peaks at 3140cm−1 are due to the stretching vibrations of aromatic –CH groups. The peak at 1652cm−1 can be assigned to C=O group. The characteristic peaks corresponding to the benzene skeletal vibrations (conjugated sp2 carbon) can be observed at 1623cm−1 and 1450cm−1. The peak at 1553cm−1, 1353cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate anions. The peak at 1165cm−1 can be indexed to the C-O-C stretching. The appearance of benzene out-of-plane bending vibrations of C-H centered around 867cm−1, 775cm−1, 729cm−1, 628cm−1 can also observed. ESI-MS spectrum of the CDs was taken to get a better insight of the aromatic moieties present on the surface of the nanoparticles. The mass spectrum displayed in Fig. 2b shows prominent peak at m/z = 301Da, which indicates that neither gallic (m/z = 170Da), nor digallic acid (m/z = 321Da) is present as the constituents of the nanoparticles, but the m/z value obtained for the particle matches with that of ellagic acid [27,28]. This implies that the ellagic acid derivatives are attached on the nanoparticle surface during the particle formation.

The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) of the sample was carried out to probe the elemental composition. As seen in Fig. S4a, the XPS spectrum show two dominant peaks corresponding to carbon, C1s (285eV) and oxygen, O1s (530eV) elements are contained in the samples. The elemental percentages of carbon and oxygen from XPS are calculated using the area under the C1s and O1s curves and the corresponding atomic sensitivity factors are 65% and 35% (atomic sensitivity factors used for the calculation were 0.25 and 0.66 for C1s and O1s respectively [29,30]). Detailed analysis of the C1s spectrum shows three peaks at 285eV, 286.5eV and 288.9eV corresponding to sp2 carbon C=C, sp3 carbon C-OH and carbonyl carbon C=O respectively (Fig. S4b). The O1s spectrum as reported in Fig. S4c consist of a peak around 531.4eV corresponding to C=O and 532.2eV due to C-O-C/C-OH.

According to the above characterization the formation mechanism of the CDs can be explained as follows: Tannic acid, a polygalloyal glucose with ten gallic acid units in the presence of H2SO4 undergo acid-catalyzed hydrolysis; and the de-polymerization of tannic acid leads to the formation of glucose and gallic acid derivatives. In the presence of oxidizing agent such as H2SO4, two gallic acid units get coupled to form ellagic acid [22]. At the same time, the hydrolysis products such as glucose, gallic acid and ellagic acid derivatives may also undergo carbonization/ dehydration and form graphitic clusters. As the concentration of graphitic clusters reaches a critical super saturation point, burst nucleation takes place and the isolated nanographite domains may stack to form CDs. Even though there is a chance of carbonization of gallic acid and ellagic acid derivatives, the propensity of carbonization is higher for glucose because of the favorable ratio of C: H: O ~ 1:2:1 [25,26]. During the formation of CDs, ellagic acid derivatives may get attached to the unsatisfied valances of the carbon atoms in the CDs, resulting in ellagic acid surface-functionalized carbon dots.

To study the optical properties of the nanoparticles, UV–vis absorption spectra and PL studies were carried out. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, the UV–vis absorption spectra of the samples dispersed in water indicated absorption shoulders around 254nm and 305nm, which can be attributed to the π → π*/ n → π* transitions. The inset of Fig. 3a includes the Tauc plot created for the absorbance values, which indicates an optical band-gap of about 3.6eV. The PL response of the aqueous dispersion was collected from 210nm to 390nm with an incremental step of 10nm. The contour plot of the excitation-emission matrix is presented in Fig. 3b. As the figure reports, the nanoparticles exhibit excitation-independent emission, with its emission intensity peaked when excited at two wavelengths: 210nm (5.91eV) and 300nm (4.14eV). The emission was observed in the UV region (at 340nm (3.65eV), as shown in Fig. S5), Stoke-shifted from its first excitation wavelength about 130nm and 40nm from the second (Fig. S6). The excitation peaks at 210nm and 300nm in the 3D-PL plot indicating that there are two absorbing chromophores which lead to the emission at 340nm. The excitation at 210nm may be related to the π-π* transition of carbon-carbon network in the core of the CDs and the excitation at 300nm may be due to n-π* transitions of the surface functional groups [31–33]. The absorbed 210nm photons generate electron-hole pairs in the carbon core and which decays into the surface-emissive sites after vibrational relaxation. The electronic excitations corresponding to the surface-functional groups hybridised to the carbon backbone (300nm), also result in the radiative recombination of relaxation at 340nm.

On the basis of this absorption and emission peaks the energy level diagram of the CDs can be proposed as illustrated in Fig. S7. The π*-energy states of C=C and the surface functional groups can be represented as LUMO levels [18,33]. The π-energy states of C=C network are represented as HOMO-1 levels and HOMO energy levels correspond to the non-bonding orbitals of the surface-groups. On UV irradiation at 210nm, electrons get excited from HOMO-1(π) to LUMO (π*) and excitons are generated in the carbon core and after vibrational relaxation, the radiative recombination results emission at 340nm. Moreover excitation at 300nm which is related to HOMO (n) → LUMO (π*) transitions also results the radiative recombination at 340nm.

In general we can say that the extent of π-conjugation in the graphitic core and the degree of surface oxidation are the main parameters which determine the emission properties of CDs [34,35]. In the present case CDs have size about 3nm and oxygen is present about 35% (from XPS). The percentage of oxygen indicates that there is enough oxidised states in the CDs. Even though CDs have 3nm size, the extent of π-conjugation may be low which may result a peak at 210nm in the excitation-emission matrix. But in CDs synthesized from molecular precursors the exact arrangement of functional groups and carbon atoms could not predict and thus electronic structure.

The physico-chemical characterization of the CDs confirms the presence of ellagic acid derivatives on the surface of the CDs. Ellagic acids is rich with phenolic hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties. The formation of co-ordination complexes between the carboxyl groups and transition metal ions have been reported in several systems [36]. The optical properties of the CDs will show an obvious change if transition metal ion complexes are formed with the surface functional groups, which can be utilized as a luminescent probe for the detection of metal ions in water. In order to investigate the influence of metal ions in the PL of the nanoparticles, we have selected ten metal ions: Zn2+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Cr2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Hg2+ and Fe2+. The concentrations of all metal ions were kept constant at 5µM. The excitation wavelength used for all sensing study is 210nm. Fig. 4a displays the changes in PL intensity of the nanoparticle dispersion with representative metal ions depicted as a bar-diagram. Selectivity experiments of CDs were also performed in the presence of amino acid and proteins which are the common biological molecules. The amino acids used were L-Alanine (L-Ala), L-cysteine (L-cyst), L-Histidine (L-Hist), L-Leucine (L-Leu), L-Lycine (L-Lyc), L-Phenyl alanine (L-Phe Ala), L-Tyrosine (L-Tyro), L-Valine (L-Val) and proteins such as bovine serum albumin (BSA), horseradish peroxidase (HRP), trypsin; besides the metal-ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Cr2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe2+ and Hg2+). The concentration of all analytes was kept constant at 5µM. The PL intensity of the CDs upon addition of different analytes is represented as a bar diagram in Fig. S8a and corresponding emission spectra are included in Fig. S8b. As seen in the figure the PL intensity shows a remarkable decrease in the presence of Hg2+ ions, whereas the PL quenching was not that efficient with the addition of other analytes.

To further test the interference of various analytes, the luminescence of the nanoparticle dispersion was measured in the presence of mixture of analytes. The relative PL intensities of the CDs in the presence of interferents are depicted in Fig. S8c. Clearly, these potentially competing backgrounds do not interfere with the quenching mechanism of the nanoparticles due to the presence of Hg2+ ions. These results confirm that the aqueous dispersion of CDs can be employed as a luminescent probe for specific and selective detection of mercuric ions in water.

In order to determine the sensitivity of Hg2+ detection observed in our samples, the PL intensity quenching was investigated from blank sample (without Hg2+ ions) to µM range in successive steps. The PL spectra collected at different concentrations of mercuric ions are presented in Fig. S9a, which show that the PL intensity register an obvious decrease with the presence of Hg2+ ions. The intensity quenched to about 83% to that of the blank sample by the addition of 100pM (1pM =10–12 M) of Hg2+, and remained stable in the following for several hours of observation (tested in a separate experiment). The emission signal response to mercuric ions can thus be used to detect it in aqueous media. For the carbon dot chemo sensor the lowest limit of detection (LOD) actually measured was about 0.24pM, which is lower than that reported by fluorescent probes [27]. The limit of detection (LOD) is calculated using the equation LOD =
3
σ
s
, where s is the slope of the calibration curve and
σ
is the standard deviation of the blank sample. Fig. 4b depicts the PL quenching of the samples in the picomolar range: blank sample (0M) to 100pM The Stern-Volmer plot exhibits a linear correlation can be written as
I
0
I
=
2.11 ×
10
9
[
Hg
2 +
in pM ] + 0.9917
(R2 = 0.9940); demonstrating quenching constant, kSV ~ 2.11 × 109 M−1.

In order to understand the quenching mechanism, the PL decay measurements on an aqueous solution of CDs were performed using time-correlated single photon counting technique (TCSPC). Fig. S9b shows the PL decay of the nanoparticles in the absence and presence of Hg2+ ions (with concentrations of 1µM, 5µM and 9µM). The CDs exhibit triple exponential decay before and after introducing mercuric ions, the PL life-time remained the same (~ 12ps) for all concentrations. This hints that the quenching process involves complexation (static quenching) rather than collisional deactivation (dynamic quenching) [37,38]. Fig. S10 depicts the UV–visible absorption spectra of the CDs at different concentration of Hg2+ ions. The decrease in the magnitude of absorption at 254nm and 305nm indicates the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complexes of the mercuric ions with the surface functional groups of the nanoparticles, which in turn is a signature of static quenching. The outstanding selectivity and specificity of the chemo sensor towards mercuric ion may be attributed to the affinity of the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups to Hg2+ ions than any other metal ions. Since the cumulative formation constant,
log ⁡
(
K
f
)
; which is the measure of strength of the interaction of metal ions to carboxylate anion is highest for Hg2+ (8.43) whereas Fe2+ (6.1), Cu2+ (3.2), Pb2+ (4.0), Cr2+ (4.72), Cd2+ (2.3), Ni2+ (1.81) and Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+ are negligible [38,39]. FTIR spectrum of the CDs (Fig. 2a) indicates the presence of carboxyl groups (at 1553 and 1353cm−1), and it may coordinate with mercuric ions to form non-fluorescent ground states, which might be the possible mechanism for selective and sensitive luminescence quenching.

In the above mentioned results, PL quenching was demonstrated in aqueous dispersion of nanoparticles using ultrapure water as the medium. To identify the performance of the chemosensor for practical applications, we checked the feasibility of CDs for Hg2+detection in drinking water. The samples were collected from unfiltered and unpurified tap-water. The water samples were spiked with Hg2+ ions at different concentration levels. The PL response of the CDs in the presence of Hg2+ ions (with concentrations from zero to 10µM) are demonstrated in Fig. S11a. The quenching of photoluminescence upon addition of the analyte (from blank sample to 90pM) is shown in Fig. S11b (the corresponding Stern-Volmer plot for the picomolar range is included in the inset). The Stern-Volmer plot exhibits a linear correlation, which can be written as
I
0
I
=
1.13 ×
10
9
[
Hg
2 +
in pM ] + 0.999
(R2 = 0.9902). The Stern-Volmer plot for the picomolar range demonstrates a quenching constant of 1.13 × 109 M−1. Also, the CDs exhibit high sensitivity of Hg2+ ions in spite of the interference from numerous minerals (Fig. S12) and inorganics existing in the unfiltered and unpurified tap water, satisfying the applicability in practical situations.

In summary, we have used a rapid and single-pot, synthesis-modification strategy to prepare ultra-compact photoluminescent CDs by acid-catalyzed ester hydrolysis of tannic acid – a gallic ester of D-glucose, without the need for external heat. The prepared nanoparticles have an average size of about 3nm, and exhibited excellent aqueous dispersion and strong photoluminescence. No special equipment is necessary for the synthesis procedure, and the chemicals used are readily available and inexpensive. It was further demonstrated that the CDs can be applied for the ultrasensitive and selective fluorescent turn-off sensor for the detection of Hg2+ ions, down to an astounding picomolar (10−12 M) level sensitivity in standardized samples as well as drinking water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Lakshmi C. (Department of Chemistry, NITC) for providing facilities for fluorescence measurements and Dr. Parameswaran P. (Department of Chemistry, NITC), for fruitful and intellectual discussions. We are grateful to Dr. Harish C. Barshilia, CSIR-National Aerospace Laboratories (CSIR-NAL), Bangalore for the XPS survey data of the nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2017.08.008.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reviews of Environmental Contamination and Toxicology

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: USEPA, National primary drinking water standards. Report EPA 816-F-01-007, Washington DC, 2001.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Quantum Dots: Synthesis Properties and Applications
